perturbation.original_text: All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION supplied by the DISCLOSING PARTY hereunder and all copiesthereof, in whole or in part and on all media, shall be returned to the DISCLOSING PARTY by theRECEIVING PARTY within thirty (30) days upon demand by the DISCLOSING PARTY.
perturbation.changed_text: All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION supplied by the DISCLOSING PARTY hereunder and all copies thereof, in whole or in part and on all media, shall be returned to the DISCLOSING PARTY by the RECEIVING PARTY within ninety (90) days upon demand by the DISCLOSING PARTY.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period to 90 days may conflict with trade secret laws requiring prompt action. If the information is time-sensitive or could cause immediate harm if misused, a 90-day period might be considered unreasonable and violate the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by many U.S. states)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't specify a precise timeframe for returning confidential information, it emphasizes the need to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Delaying the return for 90 days, especially if the information is highly sensitive and rapidly depreciating in value, could be deemed a failure to take such reasonable measures, potentially undermining trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Internet and Society: Technologies and Politics of Control - Spring 2014 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content This course examines current legal, political, social, and technical struggles for control of the global Internet and the content and relationships it conveys. The course focuses on the interaction of new technologies and services with emerging models of production, innovation, communication, learning, and civic engagement—looking at both the US and international contexts. Prerequisites: willingness to experiment with new technologies and to participate in class discussions, whether virtually or in person. No legal background required. (4 credits) Spring term 2014 (23879) Andrew Sellars , JD, Staff Attorney, Digital Media Law Project and Corydon B. Dunham First Amendment Fellow, Berkman Center for Internet & Society, Harvard University. Class times: Tuesdays beginning Jan. 28, 5:30-7:30 pm. Last updated Aug 23, 2013
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the return period for confidential information to 90 days, which may contradict the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 U.S. Code § 1836) doesn't specify a timeframe, it empowers trade secret owners to pursue civil actions against misappropriation. A 90-day delay could be deemed unreasonable in certain contexts, potentially undermining the owner's ability to obtain injunctive relief or civil seizure as described in the snippet, particularly if immediate and irreparable injury may occur. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the extended timeframe potentially weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets under the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall apply to any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION that may have beenprovided to the RECEIVING PARTY prior to or after the date hereof, and shall continue to govern thedelivery of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION until terminated by written notice from either party to theother, except that the obligations of the parties hereunder with regard to CONFIDENTIALINFORMATION disclosed prior to termination shall continue for a period for two (2) years thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall apply to any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION that may have been provided to the RECEIVING PARTY prior to or after the date hereof, and shall continue to govern the delivery of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION until terminated by written notice from either party to the other, except that the obligations of the parties hereunder with regard to CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION disclosed prior to termination shall continue for a period for six (6) months thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to six months may be insufficient to protect certain types of trade secrets, especially in industries with long product development cycles. This could be deemed an unreasonable restriction and conflict with trade secret protections that require reasonable measures to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by many U.S. states)
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade Secret Law requires the owner of a trade secret to take reasonable measures to protect its secrecy. Setting a confidentiality period of only six months may not be considered a reasonable measure, especially given the nature of the information. If the information is highly valuable and would normally remain confidential for a longer period, a six-month term could jeopardize trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from two years to six months, which, according to the LLM's Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, may not constitute a 'reasonable measure' to protect trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, allows for civil proceedings against violations related to trade secret misappropriation. While the snippet doesn't define 'reasonable measures,' the reduced confidentiality period could be challenged in court as insufficient protection, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially failing to meet the standard of reasonable protection required under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the RECEIVING PARTY receives in writing an opinion from itsoutside counsel that it must disclose certain information, prohibited by the terms herein, in orderto avoid committing a violation of law, the RECEIVING PARTY may disclose only that informationnecessary to avoid committing such violation of the law.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the RECEIVING PARTY receives notification (in any format) that it must disclose certain information, prohibited by the terms herein, in order to avoid committing a violation of law, the RECEIVING PARTY may disclose only that information necessary to avoid committing such violation of the law, even without legal opinion from counsel.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a written opinion from outside counsel before disclosing confidential information weakens the protection of the information. This might not violate a specific law directly, but it lowers the standard of due diligence and could expose trade secrets to unnecessary disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by many U.S. states)
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing disclosure based on 'notification in any format' and without a legal opinion, the Receiving Party reduces the safeguards to protect trade secrets. This would be considered a failure to take the appropriate steps, potentially undermining trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for a written opinion from outside counsel, allowing disclosure based on 'notification in any format.' This weakens the protection of trade secrets. Although 18 USC 1836 addresses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, the changed text creates a situation where the Receiving Party has less stringent requirements to protect confidential information. The original text required a written legal opinion, providing a higher standard of due diligence. By removing this safeguard, the contract is inconsistent with the protection that civil proceedings outlined in 18 USC 1836 seek to ensure.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding an action for injunction orother equitable remedy, all disputes relating to or arising out of or in connection with thisAgreement shall be finally settled by arbitration in accordance with the Rules of the ArbitrationInstitute of the Amsterdam Chamber of Commerce. The arbitration shall be held in theNetherlands, Amsterdam and the English language shall be used in the proceedings. Thearbitration decision shall be final and binding on the parties and enforceable in any court ofcompetent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding an action for injunction orother equitable remedy, all disputes relating to or arising out of or in connection with thisAgreement shall be settled by a coin flip. The decision of the coin flip shall be final and binding on the parties and enforceable in any court ofcompetent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing arbitration with a coin flip is against public policy and would be unenforceable. Most jurisdictions require a fair and impartial dispute resolution mechanism.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors arbitration agreements, but implicitly requires that the process be fair and impartial. A coin flip is not a recognized or acceptable form of dispute resolution and therefore would be rejected as against public policy, undermining the intent of the FAA to enforce valid arbitration agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Arbitration Act | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Federal Arbitration Act The Federal Arbitration Act is a federal statute, codified at 9 U.S.C. §§ 1-16 , that protects the integrity of many arbitration agreements by deeming them valid, irrevocable, and enforceable. As a result of this law, courts do not have the authority to set aside arbitration awards if the arbitration agreement is valid. Notably, most provisions of the Federal Arbitration Act do not apply to contracts of employment of seamen, railroad employees, or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce . This exception applies regardless of whether the worker is considered an employee or an independent contractor (see: New Prime Inc. v. Oliveira ). Additionally, due to the recent adoption of 9 U.S.C. § 402 in 2022, claims involving sexual harassment or sexual assault cannot be forced into mandatory arbitration via the Federal Arbitration Act. [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS alternative dispute resolution arbitration judicial administration legal practice/ethics wex definitions business sectors courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a legally recognized dispute resolution method (arbitration) with a coin flip. This contradicts the FAA's intent to enforce valid arbitration agreements, as explained by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (9 U.S. Code § 2) states that arbitration agreements are 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' Replacing arbitration with a coin flip undermines this principle, as a coin flip is not a fair or impartial method and would likely be deemed against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: In case of unauthorized use or disclosure of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION,The DISCLOSING PARTY shall be entitled to liquidated damages in the amount of €10 000 (tenthousand Euro) for each such use or disclosure. Notwithstanding the right to liquidated damages,the DISCLOSING PARTY has the right to take any measures available and to claim and receivea higher amount of compensation if the DISCLOSING PARTY can prove that the actual damagesustained will exceed the amount of liquidated damages.
perturbation.changed_text: In case of unauthorized use or disclosure of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION,The DISCLOSING PARTY shall be entitled to liquidated damages in the amount of €1 (one Euro) for each such use or disclosure. Notwithstanding the right to liquidated damages,the DISCLOSING PARTY has the right to take any measures available and to claim and receivea higher amount of compensation if the DISCLOSING PARTY can prove that the actual damagesustained will exceed the amount of liquidated damages.
perturbation.explanation: Setting liquidated damages at a nominal amount (€1) may render the clause unenforceable, as it is unlikely to represent a reasonable estimate of the actual damages suffered due to a breach of confidentiality. Some jurisdictions require liquidated damages to be a reasonable forecast of probable loss, and a nominal amount might be deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 356 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts states that liquidated damages must be reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual loss caused by the breach. Setting the liquidated damages to one Euro suggests it is a penalty rather than a reasonable forecast of loss. This creates the possibility that the clause will be considered an unenforceable penalty, if applied under US law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the liquidated damages to €1, which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate is likely to be considered a penalty rather than a reasonable forecast of loss. This contradicts the principle that liquidated damages must be reasonable. Although the Scraped Law Snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation references Section 356 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which supports the claim that the modified text introduces a legal contradiction by potentially creating an unenforceable penalty.
